title: Election Night

jokes.text: History is calling, and it won't go to voicemail.
jokes.setup: Solemn election night moment; staffer is trying to inspire Selina with gravitas.
jokes.punchline: Deflates historic rhetoric with a mundane modern tech reference — voicemail.
jokes.explanation: A tight, well-constructed joke that takes the grandiosity of 'history calling' literally and undercuts it with a throwaway modern detail. Good economy, lands cleanly as an opener.

jokes.text: America doesn't just love you, ma'am, she is in love with you. / We've lost Kentucky!
jokes.setup: Gary delivers an over-the-top romantic declaration of America's devotion to Selina.
jokes.punchline: Immediately cut by someone announcing they've lost Kentucky — brutal comic juxtaposition.
jokes.explanation: Structurally near-perfect: Gary's sycophantic hyperbole is immediately demolished by cold electoral reality. The timing and juxtaposition is the essence of Veep's comedy — inflated ego vs. deflating facts. Rewatch bonus because you can see it coming in retrospect and it's still funny.

jokes.text: Amy, if he wins, our lobbying stock is gonna droop like a chimp's tits.
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy discuss their post-election career positioning.
jokes.punchline: The image of 'a chimp's tits' for sagging professional prospects is grotesquely vivid and very Dan.
jokes.explanation: Highly original simile that feels character-specific to Dan's crude pragmatism. 'Droop like a chimp's tits' is memorably disgusting and precise — Dan would say this. Strong quotability.

jokes.text: Well, on air I'm gonna keep in with her. Off air I'm telling the Nazis that she's hiding in the attic.
jokes.setup: Media pundit explains their dual strategy of appearing neutral while hedging bets.
jokes.punchline: The Nazi/attic metaphor for political backstabbing is shockingly dark and reframes betrayal in the most extreme historical register possible.
jokes.explanation: Audaciously dark metaphor for political duplicity. Works because the logic is internally consistent — hiding in the attic, Nazis finding you — it's not random, it's a coherent (if extreme) extended metaphor for betrayal. Iannucci-level darkness.

jokes.text: So, suck it, Fox.
jokes.setup: Anchor introduces a statistics expert who accurately predicted every midterm Senate seat margin.
jokes.punchline: After the impressive credential, delivers a childish 'suck it, Fox' — total tonal whiplash.
jokes.explanation: Quick, punchy, works through tonal contrast. Not a standout but efficiently funny.

jokes.text: Hey, wait, it's the testicle man. Guilty as charged. Check 'em, don't neglect 'em.
jokes.setup: Jonah enters the TV studio area; anchor recognizes him from a men's health campaign.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's entire public identity has collapsed to being 'the testicle man' — he owns it with his catchphrase.
jokes.explanation: Deeply character-specific — Jonah's arc of humiliation crystallized into a catchphrase. 'Check 'em, don't neglect 'em' is genuinely catchy and awful. Works because Jonah is obliviously proud.

jokes.text: This whole scrotum situation is really working out for you, sir.
jokes.setup: Jonah explains the testicle campaign came out of a terrible Senate hearing.
jokes.punchline: Being told the scrotum situation is 'working out' for him is deadpan absurdism — his worst moment became his brand.
jokes.explanation: Clean economy — 'this whole scrotum situation' is a delightfully absurd phrase delivered with complete sincerity. Good escalation of the Jonah/testicle running gag.

jokes.text: That hearing was terrible, but it really got my face out there, you know?
jokes.setup: Jonah admits the hearing was terrible.
jokes.punchline: His silver lining is pure Jonah obliviousness — being nationally humiliated is reframed as good personal branding.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly in-character for Jonah's oblivious narcissism. The joke is entirely in the delivery — his cheerful lack of self-awareness.

jokes.text: You're the face of workplace bullying and genital health.
jokes.setup: Jonah says the hearing 'got his face out there.'
jokes.punchline: The specific combination of 'workplace bullying AND genital health' as a personal brand is the punchline — the juxtaposition of those two things is perfectly absurd.
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the Jonah sequence. 'The face of workplace bullying and genital health' is an absurdly precise summary of Jonah's entire arc — two things that should never share a sentence, delivered with complete deadpan. Highly quotable, perfectly economical, and earned through seasons of character work. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: Oh, man, election nights are my cocaine. It used to be election nights and cocaine were my cocaine, but...
jokes.setup: Ben expresses enthusiasm for election night.
jokes.punchline: The self-correction reveals actual cocaine was previously in the mix — the trailing 'but...' implies sobriety or reduced capacity, making the original enthusiasm already a step down.
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent: the setup plants the metaphor, then the self-correction reveals it was literal. The dangling 'but...' is the real joke — implying a tragic sobriety or depleted capacity. Deeply character-specific to Ben's world-weary, used-up political operative persona. Among the best-constructed jokes in the episode. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: If Fox says it's close, then it must be. Then again, they said the Rapture was close.
jokes.setup: Fox News reporting the election is close.
jokes.punchline: Undercutting Fox's credibility by comparing their election forecasting to predicting the Rapture — both things that haven't happened.
jokes.explanation: Solid media satire joke, well-constructed with a good self-correction structure. A bit of a stock media joke but executed well.

jokes.text: Okay, settle down. A bowl of hair could win those states.
jokes.setup: Staffers cheer loudly for Vermont and Connecticut wins.
jokes.punchline: Dismisses those states as meaninglessly safe Democratic territory — 'a bowl of hair' is a brilliantly specific image of an inert, meaningless candidate.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'A bowl of hair' as an image of a non-candidate is strikingly original — it's not just 'anyone could win it' but a hilariously specific non-entity. Maximum funny per word, delivered with Ben's characteristic deflating authority. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Well, most people get just two.
jokes.setup: Jonah complains about only getting four seconds with the president.
jokes.punchline: The reassurance that four seconds is actually generous — most get two — underlines how thoroughly unimportant everyone is to Selina.
jokes.explanation: Good quick beat that extends the Jonah humiliation efficiently. The specificity of 'two seconds' is funny.

jokes.text: I'm saving that for when I go to prison.
jokes.setup: Staffer mentions projections arriving; Bill says he hasn't read them yet.
jokes.punchline: Bill is calmly preparing prison reading material — the casual acceptance of his probable imprisonment is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy. Bill's equanimity about going to prison — treating it as a settled fact he's just logistically preparing for — is both character-specific and genuinely funny. Economy is superb.

jokes.text: Even money they give you the chair.
jokes.setup: Bill mentions saving something for prison.
jokes.punchline: Ben casually ups the stakes to execution with total deadpan.
jokes.explanation: Quick escalation of the prison joke — Ben casually suggesting the electric chair is perfectly dark and brief.

jokes.text: I had to ditch my fiancé. I'll be thinking about that while I'm with whichever guy makes me his prison fiancé.
jokes.setup: Bill says no one came out of the hearings unscathed.
jokes.punchline: The elegant parallel structure — lost a fiancé, will gain a prison fiancé — is both dark and structurally satisfying.
jokes.explanation: Structurally one of the best jokes in the episode. The chiastic structure (lost fiancé → gain prison fiancé) is genuinely elegant. 'Whichever guy makes me his prison fiancé' is both horrifying and oddly resigned — Bill has clearly processed this. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor in construction quality.

jokes.text: Gary's 'Fuck!' reaction to Karen being Selina's invited friend.
jokes.setup: Selina cheerfully mentions she asked a friend to be with her tonight — Karen.
jokes.punchline: Gary's visceral 'Fuck!' followed by covering it as a cramp. His jealousy over Selina's personal connections is a running character beat.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly character-specific. Gary's wounded jealousy when Selina has any other personal relationship is one of the show's great running jokes. The 'cramp' cover is instantly recognizable as Gary lying desperately.

jokes.text: That was transcendent bullshitting.
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan have just delivered a flawless double-act of saying nothing informative on TV about the election.
jokes.punchline: Amy's genuine admiration for the quality of their mutual evasion — 'transcendent bullshitting' — is both self-aware and a perfect description of TV punditry.
jokes.explanation: Sharp media satire with great economy. 'Transcendent bullshitting' as a phrase is both precise and quotable — it's the show critiquing political punditry while also being delighted by it.

jokes.text: You're gonna win in a slide. You know, marginally.
jokes.setup: Kent tries to reassure Selina she's going to win.
jokes.punchline: Immediately corrects 'landslide' down to 'slide,' then further corrects to 'marginally' — the retreating qualifier is perfectly Kent, data-driven to the point of uselessness as encouragement.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. Kent is constitutionally incapable of offering unconditional reassurance — his data brain won't let him say 'landslide' when 'marginal' is more accurate. The two-step self-correction is structurally clean and deeply in character. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Ah! These damn hotel carpets. I keep getting that static electric shock. You need one of those ionic bracelets that grounds you.
jokes.setup: Tense election night atmosphere; Selina is trying to read Karen for emotional support.
jokes.punchline: Karen derails any meaningful connection by going on about static electricity and ionic bracelets — her complete inability to read the room is her defining trait.
jokes.explanation: Good Karen characterization — she is relentlessly, cheerfully useless. The static electricity tangent on election night is a small but effective cringe beat.

jokes.text: It's the prison toilet situation which preys on one's mind. Defecating in full view of another man is unthinkable.
jokes.setup: Election results are coming in; the group is anxious.
jokes.punchline: Bill's mind has drifted entirely to prison logistics — specifically the open toilet. The formal diction ('defecating,' 'preys on one's mind,' 'unthinkable') against the crude subject matter is the core joke.
jokes.explanation: One of Bill's best moments. The collision of Bill's patrician vocabulary with the visceral indignity of prison toilets is perfectly executed. 'Defecating in full view of another man is unthinkable' is a genuinely funny sentence — the formality is the joke.

jokes.text: Yeah, Bill, put a cork in it, will you? / You know, that actually may help.
jokes.setup: Ben tells Bill to 'put a cork in it' regarding the prison toilet concerns.
jokes.punchline: Bill takes the idiom literally — a cork might actually help with the open-toilet situation. Unintentional wordplay that Bill plays completely straight.
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay executed deadpan. Ben's idiom accidentally becomes literal advice for Bill's prison toilet anxiety. The joke requires paying attention to set it up, which gives it a satisfying payoff. Rewatch bonus because you catch it faster the second time.

jokes.text: He's like a Native American tracker. He can hear it in the wind.
jokes.setup: Kent goes silent and alert, apparently sensing a call coming before anyone else.
jokes.punchline: Ben's wry description of Kent's data-sensing ability as mystical tracker instinct.
jokes.explanation: Decent bit of texture. Ben's wry asides about Kent's weirdness are a reliable comedy vein in the show. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: I could pardon you? / That wouldn't look good. You'd be the first person to say that. / I'm not entirely sure I would in this circumstance.
jokes.setup: Bill hints he wants Selina to pardon him if she loses.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response is a masterpiece of passive denial — 'you'd be the first person to say that' (deflect) followed by 'I'm not entirely sure I would' (refuse with plausible deniability).
jokes.explanation: Great character comedy. Selina's non-answer is perfectly calibrated — she neither commits to pardoning him nor explicitly refuses, in the most Selina way possible. The two-step evasion is beautifully written.

jokes.text: Hey, Bill, I got a lot on the line with my presidency right now, so I don't really have time to be thinking about your whatever the fuck it is. / Probable imprisonment.
jokes.setup: Bill keeps pushing for reassurance about his legal situation.
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses his 'probable imprisonment' as a minor distraction from her own concerns. Bill supplying the phrase 'probable imprisonment' helpfully after she trails off is the button.
jokes.explanation: Strong character beat. Selina's self-absorption is the joke — Bill's probable imprisonment is just a distraction. His helpful completion of her sentence is a great button.

jokes.text: Set up an office with Selina at the Betty Ford Clinic? No, I've applied for a job outside. Politics bores me. It bores the living hell out of me. God, it's boring.
jokes.setup: Someone asks Amy what she'll do if O'Brien wins.
jokes.punchline: The Betty Ford joke lands first; then Amy's escalating insistence that politics is boring — in a talking head confessional, clearly filmed mid-crisis — is the real joke. The triple iteration ('bores me / bores the living hell / God, it's boring') is comic escalation.
jokes.explanation: Good Amy confessional — the Betty Ford line is sharp, and her insistence politics is boring while clearly living for it is a neat irony. Escalating repetition works.

jokes.text: Amy looks off her game, distracted. I only really know Amy as the woman who rushed everywhere clutching her phone like it contained her frozen embryos.
jokes.setup: Dan is doing a confessional about Amy's performance on TV.
jokes.punchline: The frozen embryos image for Amy's frantic phone-clutching is both a perfect description of her and an unexpectedly vivid and specific metaphor.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'Clutching her phone like it contained her frozen embryos' is an unusually vivid, specific, and original image. It says something true about Amy's anxious, workaholic character while being extremely funny. Strong quotability. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Works frantically to avoid dealing with her weird mix of lack of self-worth and narcissism. I really like her.
jokes.setup: Dan continues his psychological analysis of Amy.
jokes.punchline: After a genuinely cutting diagnosis of Amy's psyche, 'I really like her' lands as an absurd non-sequitur — Dan's affection is completely at odds with his clinical dissection of her.
jokes.explanation: Excellent button on a confessional. The precision of 'weird mix of lack of self-worth and narcissism' is Veep's analytical cruelty at its best, and 'I really like her' as a kicker is a perfect unexpected turn. Structure is superb. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Uh, ma'am, I don't mean to be the bearer of bad news, but... [pause] Called Wisconsin. Projected as an O'Brien win. That was the bad news, which in the end I didn't bear.
jokes.setup: Staffer prefaces bad news with 'I don't mean to be the bearer of bad news.'
jokes.punchline: After delivering it anyway, he notes the paradox: 'That was the bad news, which in the end I didn't bear' — a reflexively logical observation that he technically did bear it.
jokes.explanation: A tight joke that turns on the literal meaning of a cliché. The self-correction ('which in the end I didn't bear') is pleasingly nerdy and unexpected.

jokes.text: No. Um, a lot of the polls have not closed yet. So, no. No, you know, it's no.
jokes.setup: On-air anchor asks Amy if the race is slipping away from Meyer.
jokes.punchline: Amy's spiral of 'no's becomes increasingly unconvincing — visibly not believing herself, the accumulation of 'no' variants is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Strong cringe performance beat. Amy's loyalty to Selina colliding with her knowledge that O'Brien is winning produces a beautifully unconvincing cascade of denials. Character-specific and earned.

jokes.text: O'Brien can't be president. He's still contracted to be the KFC logo.
jokes.setup: People are saying O'Brien might win.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's objection is that O'Brien has a contract with KFC — a completely made-up, absurdist obstacle to the presidency.
jokes.explanation: Delightfully absurd. Jonah's brain creates a wholly invented contractual obstacle to democracy. The specificity of 'KFC logo' (not just 'KFC spokesman') elevates it. Very Jonah.

jokes.text: Karen being asked a hypothetical question and taking a full 30 seconds to say essentially nothing.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Karen directly: should Selina and Tom have swapped roles for the election?
jokes.punchline: Karen's response is 'oh, well, you're asking me a hypothetical question and I need to answer it as best I can' — she has said literally nothing. Selina's 'Okay, well, you just did' is the perfect button.
jokes.explanation: One of the best Karen moments in the series. Her evasion is so complete and so confidently delivered that it becomes performance art. Selina's 'Okay, well, you just did' is a flawless button. Character integration is a 9 — Karen is entirely defined by this useless non-commitment. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: I'm going. I have to go. / What? No, you can't do that. / Yeah, no, I have to see this play out with Selina.
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy are on live TV as election pundits; Amy suddenly decides she has to leave mid-broadcast.
jokes.punchline: Amy's loyalty to Selina overrides her professional obligations in real time, and her explanation ('I've spent a third of my life leading up to this night') makes it both funny and slightly sad.
jokes.explanation: Good character moment. Amy's single-minded loyalty manifesting as abandoning a live broadcast is very her — and Dan's shock that she's actually leaving is a nice reaction beat.

jokes.text: Some of our viewers were complaining that you're shrill anyway.
jokes.setup: Greg 'graciously' accepts Amy leaving mid-broadcast.
jokes.punchline: He undermines his acceptance by mentioning viewer complaints about Amy being shrill — a sexist media insult deployed as a parting shot.
jokes.explanation: Sharp media-world satire. 'Shrill' as a coded gendered insult is pointed, and Amy's escalating offense at it — 'I'm never shrill' — turns it into a good cringe escalation.

jokes.text: Amy's on-air meltdown: 'And you're acting as though I'm sounding shrill right now? / Yeah, yeah, keep making that face implying that I'm shrill. / Hey, could someone check the parking lot? I think all the alarms are going off.'
jokes.setup: Amy objects to being called shrill.
jokes.punchline: Her furious denial of being shrill makes her progressively shriller, culminating in Dan's parking lot alarm line — which is perfectly timed and devastating.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best sequences. The structure is perfect: the more Amy denies being shrill, the shriller she gets, and Dan's callback ('could someone check the parking lot') is the ideal punchline — cruel, precise, and perfectly timed. The escalation here is textbook Veep. Dan's line is both devastating and genuinely funny. Justifies the high scores.

jokes.text: Sorry for your loss, ma'am. I mean about Wisconsin, not a person.
jokes.setup: Jonah awkwardly condoles Selina on Wisconsin.
jokes.punchline: His clarification that he means Wisconsin and not a dead person is a perfect Jonah moment — he creates a problem that didn't exist and then clumsily solves it.
jokes.explanation: Compact Jonah moment. His self-conscious clarification creates the very awkwardness he was trying to avoid — very characteristic.

jokes.text: Ow! Fuck! I forgot. I have incompatible shoes with the rug.
jokes.setup: Jonah exits after the awkward condolence.
jokes.punchline: Jonah gets a static shock from the carpet — his own earlier conversation about ionic bracelets comes back as slapstick karma.
jokes.explanation: Good structural callback — Jonah suffers the exact thing Karen mentioned earlier. The line 'incompatible shoes with the rug' is an absurdly precise and wrong explanation. Rewatch bonus for the callback.

jokes.text: Screw this whole sloppy, backseat blowjob of a night anyway. I don't give a shit.
jokes.setup: Results are going badly; Selina is frustrated.
jokes.punchline: 'Backseat blowjob of a night' as a descriptor for a chaotic, undignified election night is a very Selina insult — simultaneously vulgar and specific.
jokes.explanation: Very Selina — her insults tend to be crude but weirdly specific. 'Sloppy backseat blowjob' has a strong image quality that makes it memorable. Good character integration.

jokes.text: The Wisconsin call has been rescinded. / Rescinded is actually not a word that's immediately clear. / This is a 360-degree turn. / It's 180.
jokes.setup: A staffer announces the Wisconsin call has been rescinded — good news.
jokes.punchline: Two simultaneous comedy beats: (1) someone says 'rescinded' isn't immediately clear, and (2) someone calls it a '360-degree turn' — which would mean they're back where they started — corrected to '180.'
jokes.explanation: Two jokes in rapid succession, both landing. The 360-degree correction is a classic dumb-person-using-clichés error and the 'rescinded is not immediately clear' is a nice bit of pedantry.

jokes.text: Rescinded is my new favorite word!
jokes.setup: The Wisconsin call is rescinded and Ben/staffer bicker about the vocabulary.
jokes.punchline: Selina's jubilant declaration that 'rescinded' is her new favorite word — a political word of salvation.
jokes.explanation: Quick, effective beat. Selina's emotional whiplash from despair to jubilation over a single word is good character work.

jokes.text: We are back in the game. Back from the dead. Zombies, bottom of the ninth.
jokes.setup: Wisconsin is no longer called for O'Brien.
jokes.punchline: Ben's mixed metaphors — zombies + baseball's 'bottom of the ninth' — are enthusiastic and slightly incoherent, which is perfectly Ben.
jokes.explanation: Functional enthusiasm beat. The mixed metaphors are mildly funny but not sharp enough to be remarkable.

jokes.text: It appears that Matty's domino is standing up straight again.
jokes.setup: Earlier, Matty called Wisconsin the 'domino' signaling O'Brien's victory.
jokes.punchline: Greg refers to Matty's domino metaphor now — but with the Wisconsin call rescinded, the domino is 'standing up straight again,' a quietly absurd image of a domino somehow resurrecting.
jokes.explanation: Decent callback that extends Matty's domino metaphor to absurd breaking point — a domino that stands back up. Mild but clever.

jokes.text: Your website is shit. / Honest to God. Shit. / Go back to the Shire, you fucking moonfaced hobbit.
jokes.setup: Matty Curtis, the statistics expert, is being criticized after his Wisconsin call failed.
jokes.punchline: Selina and Ben pile on with 'your website is shit' and then Ben's insult — 'moonfaced hobbit' — escalates the attack to the surreal.
jokes.explanation: 'Moonfaced hobbit' is a highly original compound insult — 'moon-faced' is a real insult but adding 'hobbit' adds geographic/fantasy specificity that elevates it. The 'go back to the Shire' extension makes it complete. Strong quotability.

jokes.text: 'Oh, Tom James. Ooh, it's Tom James. He's so dreamy. Sign my tits, Tom James.'
jokes.setup: Tom James is trying to discuss his role in the administration; Selina mock-describes how everyone else perceives him.
jokes.punchline: Selina's impersonation of a fangirl — 'sign my tits, Tom James' — is both funny and revealing of her jealous contempt for his popularity.
jokes.explanation: Strong Selina line. Her jealousy and contempt for Tom James' appeal expressed through an escalating fangirl impression — 'sign my tits' is the perfect escalation from 'he's so dreamy.' Character integration is high: only Selina would say this about her own VP with this mixture of mockery and wounded pride.

jokes.text: Madam President, I don't want to be impotent. / I... I don't really... / In your administration.
jokes.setup: Tom James tries to have a serious conversation about his role; begins with 'I don't want to be impotent.'
jokes.punchline: Selina's startled reaction to 'impotent' (sexually) and Tom's clarification — 'in your administration' — is a classic misdirection beat.
jokes.explanation: Classic double-entendre setup. The misdirection is well-executed with Selina's visible confusion serving as the beat. A bit of a stock joke structurally, but the performance elevates it.

jokes.text: No, I want to be treasury secretary. / What? / As well as veep. / You want to run my economy? / Bank of Tom James.
jokes.setup: Tom asks for an important cabinet role; Selina expects something minor.
jokes.punchline: 'Bank of Tom James' — Tom delivers this with complete confidence, which makes the audacity of his request funnier. Selina's 'What?' reaction is the beat.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Tom's ambition. 'Bank of Tom James' as a delivery of a Treasury pitch is both grandiose and efficient. Tom's complete self-assurance makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Anything else you want to ask for? Want your face on money or something?
jokes.setup: Tom has just asked to be both VP and Treasury Secretary.
jokes.punchline: Selina escalates his demands to the logical extreme — putting his face on money — which is the natural endpoint of wanting to run the Treasury.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation punchline. The logical extension from 'run the economy' to 'face on money' is tight and satisfying.

jokes.text: Want that TV? Take it.
jokes.setup: Selina is sarcastic about Tom's demands, gesturing at absurd escalation.
jokes.punchline: She points at a physical object in the room — the TV — and sarcastically offers it. The deflation from grand political demands to 'take that TV' is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Three-word joke that works entirely through context. After grandiose demands, the sudden offer of a TV in the room is a hilarious scale-deflation. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: I've known sailors less likely to go either way than this.
jokes.setup: The election is still too close to call.
jokes.punchline: Comparing the election's ambiguity to sailors' sexual versatility — 'go either way' as a double meaning about election uncertainty and bisexuality.
jokes.explanation: Sharp wordplay with 'go either way' doing double duty. The naval reference is specific and elevates what could be a generic double entendre. 'Pardon my ribaldry' is a perfect period button.

jokes.text: Fuck Iowa. I'd say nuke it, but I think someone already did.
jokes.setup: Iowa is called for O'Brien.
jokes.punchline: Selina's nuclear dismissal of Iowa — and the implication it's already been nuked — is a casually brutal dismissal of a state.
jokes.explanation: Selina's contempt for states she can't win expressed as casual nuclear indifference. Good economy; the second half ('but I think someone already did') is the real joke — not just violence but preemptive desolation.

jokes.text: You might gather the general trend is that it's too close to call.
jokes.setup: Ben has listed several states that are all too close to call.
jokes.punchline: After the list, he summarizes with a statement of the obvious that's somehow funnier for being stated.
jokes.explanation: Functional deadpan. Ben's sarcastic summary of the obvious is a mild chuckle, nothing more.

jokes.text: God, I am so tense. I could crack a walnut in my ass.
jokes.setup: Selina expresses her election night stress.
jokes.punchline: 'I could crack a walnut in my ass' as a measure of tension is deeply Selina — crude, physical, and vivid.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's great lines. 'I could crack a walnut in my ass' as a measure of stress is wildly original, viscerally specific, and perfectly in Selina's register of self-deprecating crude anxiety. High quotability. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Everybody give it up for Band of the Horses. / Wait, what? / Oh, Band... Oh, I'm sorry. I'm sorry. Give it up for Band of Horses.
jokes.setup: Jonah is MCing the election rally and introducing a band.
jokes.punchline: He gets the name wrong — 'Band of the Horses' instead of 'Band of Horses' — and his flustered correction is a classic Jonah humiliation beat.
jokes.explanation: Compact Jonah humiliation. Getting the band name slightly wrong and then over-correcting is very in character. The joke is understated — just one wrong word.

jokes.text: I just want to say thank you for supporting the men's health campaign and letting me tell guys that they should feel themselves up. Easiest job I ever had.
jokes.setup: Jonah addresses the rally crowd about the men's health campaign.
jokes.punchline: 'Feel themselves up' is a deliberately crude characterization of a self-exam that Jonah seems genuinely proud of. 'Easiest job I ever had' is a great button — he's delighted to have had an easy gig.
jokes.explanation: Good running gag payoff. Jonah's characterization of testicular self-examination as 'feeling yourself up' is crudely accurate and funny. The 'easiest job' button is solid.

jokes.text: You guys remember the A-Team, right? 'I'm the A-Team.' Mr. T... they were supposed to be on the run, but they were in a really distinctive van. Yeah, what was up with that?
jokes.setup: Jonah tries to keep the rally crowd engaged with pop culture references.
jokes.punchline: His A-Team observation — that fugitives drove a distinctive van — is a genuine (if delayed) observation, but delivered with such Jonah nervousness and incompetence that the failure of the bit is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The joke is really Jonah's incompetence as an MC — he reaches for the A-Team as a reference and then can't close it. The observation about the van is mildly true but the context makes it work.

jokes.text: Feeling kind of zen-ish, you know? I realize that there's nothing more I can do. Things are out of my control and I just have to let the tension go. / Get the fuck out of here, Kent.
jokes.setup: Kent delivers a calm, zen-sounding speech about releasing tension.
jokes.punchline: Selina's immediate 'Get the fuck out of here, Kent' — the zen persona is so at odds with Kent's data-driven intensity that Selina (and the audience) refuses to accept it.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding character joke. Kent attempting zen is inherently funny because he is the least zen character in the show. The setup is his earnest delivery; the punchline is Selina's perfect refusal to engage. High character integration — depends entirely on knowing Kent. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Yeah, and he was, like, 60. / He was 35.
jokes.setup: Selina mentions Catherine's ex-boyfriend Jason being older.
jokes.punchline: Selina misremembers his age by 25 years; Catherine corrects her. Selina's casual inaccuracy about her own daughter's relationship is very Selina.
jokes.explanation: Quick but effective. Selina's obliviousness to basic facts about Catherine's life — doubling the boyfriend's age — is a small but sharp character beat.

jokes.text: You know, Catherine, men are horrible. Okay? I mean, I have to just tell it to you like it is, honey. But all men are awful.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to comfort Catherine about her breakup.
jokes.punchline: Selina's comfort is just a global condemnation of all men — which is meant to help but is also revealing of Selina's own damage. The delivery of this as wisdom is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Good mother-daughter cringe. Selina's relationship advice revealing her own bitterness while being delivered as profound wisdom is a solid character beat.

jokes.text: And the key is to just find a man who's the least horrible.
jokes.setup: Selina's ongoing 'all men are awful' speech to Catherine.
jokes.punchline: The advice bottoms out at 'find the least horrible' — not good, just least bad. Delivered with complete maternal sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Excellent button on the men-are-horrible sequence. 'Find the least horrible' as relationship advice is perfectly bleak and maximally efficient — it does everything with four words. The low bar as maternal wisdom is genuinely funny and character-defining. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Oh, look at that photo of you and me. You look like me in that picture. / No. No, uh-uh. Not at all.
jokes.setup: Selina and Catherine are having a tender mother-daughter moment.
jokes.punchline: Selina's comment that Catherine looks like her — meant as a compliment — is violently rejected by Catherine. The vehemence of 'No, uh-uh. Not at all' suggests this is a wound.
jokes.explanation: Effective cringe comedy. The tenderness punctured by Catherine's flat refusal to accept any resemblance to her mother is both funny and a little sad. Functional.

jokes.text: Oh, there's Daddy. / Horrible.
jokes.setup: Catherine spots a photo of Selina's ex-husband.
jokes.punchline: Selina's instant one-word verdict — 'Horrible' — perfectly caps her 'all men are awful' speech by applying it to her own ex.
jokes.explanation: Perfect button. One word, maximum impact. The callback to 'men are horrible' is paid off with total economy — she sees her ex's photo and just says 'Horrible.' Economy score is 10. The structure of setup (general statement) → confirmation (personal example) is textbook. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: God, it always seems longer with Catherine.
jokes.setup: Selina returns from mother-daughter time after only 20 minutes.
jokes.punchline: Her immediate complaint that time with Catherine feels interminable — said to her staff rather than Catherine — is cruel but completely in character.
jokes.explanation: Sharp and economical. Selina's relationship with Catherine expressed in one dismissive sentence. The detail that it was only 20 minutes makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Just something with real sugar. No sweeteners or I'll shit my pants.
jokes.setup: Ben asks if anyone wants something from the soda machine.
jokes.punchline: Amy's casual disclosure that artificial sweeteners cause her to defecate uncontrollably, offered as a normal dietary preference, is a TMI overshare delivered with total matter-of-factness.
jokes.explanation: Excellent understated overshare. The clinical matter-of-factness of 'or I'll shit my pants' — delivered as a normal preference — is very funny. Amy's stress and the timing make it land.

jokes.text: TV's Amy Brookheimer.
jokes.setup: Amy returns to the hotel suite after her TV meltdown.
jokes.punchline: Selina greets her as 'TV's Amy Brookheimer' — referencing her TV pundit persona while knowing she just had a live breakdown on air.
jokes.explanation: Nice callback with great economy. 'TV's Amy Brookheimer' as a title — normally used for beloved celebrities — applied to someone who just had a meltdown on live TV is perfectly deadpan.

jokes.text: Amy Brookheimer who so successfully ran my campaign until she became unwell.
jokes.setup: Selina introduces Amy to the rally crowd.
jokes.punchline: 'Until she became unwell' — a genteel euphemism for Amy's spectacular resignation/breakdown — is deployed as a public description of a colleague, with Selina seemingly oblivious to how horrible this is.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best Selina moments. Describing Amy's breakdown to a rally crowd as her 'becoming unwell' — with the primly polite Victorian-era phrasing — is savage, accurate, and completely in Selina's character. The public context makes it worse. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: These are projections. These are not real results. They're ghosts.
jokes.setup: Staff tell her the numbers suggest O'Brien has the election.
jokes.punchline: Selina insists projections are 'ghosts' — a dramatic denial of mathematical reality using supernatural metaphor.
jokes.explanation: Strong denial-mode Selina. 'They're ghosts' as a rejection of data is absurdist and revealing — it's her magical thinking expressed in a single perfect metaphor.

jokes.text: This fucking job sucks anyway.
jokes.setup: Selina decides to concede the election with dignity.
jokes.punchline: After announcing she wants dignity in defeat, she immediately adds 'This fucking job sucks anyway' — puncturing her own grace with bitter relief.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between 'I want dignity' and 'this fucking job sucks anyway' is a great structural beat. Selina reveals that her loss of face isn't just political but personal relief. Economical and completely in character. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Please don't electrocute me.
jokes.setup: Selina picks up a phone to call O'Brien and concede.
jokes.punchline: She addresses the phone with 'Please don't electrocute me' — a static shock callback and/or existential anxiety about the call, delivered to an inanimate object.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully timed and economical. Whether it's a callback to the static shock running gag or Selina's anxiety about the call manifesting as literal fear of the phone, it works on both levels. Delivered to an inanimate object with total sincerity.

jokes.text: Ben, yeah, Mike, Selina's conceding, so forget about the soda and just grab a crate of whiskey.
jokes.setup: Ben privately tells Mike the news about the concession.
jokes.punchline: Ben's immediate pivot from soda errand to 'grab a crate of whiskey' is the perfect Ben response to bad news — pragmatic self-medication.
jokes.explanation: Ben at his most Ben. The pivot from 'soda' to 'crate of whiskey' perfectly calibrates the magnitude of the news. 'Crate' (not bottle) is the right word.

jokes.text: Time to turn that noose back into a necktie, buddy.
jokes.setup: Dan calls someone with news that the Pennsylvania call may be wrong.
jokes.punchline: 'Noose back into a necktie' — the reversal of the suicide metaphor for political despair is grimly funny and very Dan.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best-constructed lines. The image of a noose being turned back into a necktie is original, specific, darkly comic, and perfectly economical. The transformation metaphor — same object, different meaning — is genuinely clever. 'Buddy' as a casual button elevates it. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Selina desperately trying not to complete the concession call she's mid-way through with O'Brien.
jokes.setup: Selina is mid-concession call when staff rush in to stop her.
jokes.punchline: She has to simultaneously keep talking to O'Brien and receive signals from her staff, leading to her improvising filler ('Uh, how's it going?') while stalling — the cringe of being caught half-conceding.
jokes.explanation: Great sustained cringe sequence. The physical comedy of Selina fielding two conversations simultaneously while trying to unsay a concession is classic Veep plotting. Her 'how's it going?' to a presidential rival she was just conceding to is an excellent beat.

jokes.text: I don't know why you would even imagine that I would be doing that, Bill.
jokes.setup: O'Brien asks if she's conceding — because she called to concede.
jokes.punchline: Selina denies she was conceding with total confidence, despite having called for exactly that purpose — the audacity of the denial is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The perfect audacity of Selina denying what was literally happening makes this land hard. Her confidence in a completely transparent lie is the core of her character.

jokes.text: I'm taking the White House. Please leave the hot tub on.
jokes.setup: Selina, now back from the brink of conceding, gets to demand things from O'Brien.
jokes.punchline: After the triumphant 'I'm taking the White House,' the second ask — 'please leave the hot tub on' — is a magnificently mundane follow-up that deflates the moment hilariously.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. The structure is perfect: 'I'm taking the White House' sets up a triumphant power move, then 'please leave the hot tub on' destroys the grandeur by revealing her first priority is a luxury amenity. It's character-perfect (Selina's vainglory and creature comforts), structurally immaculate, and highly quotable. The 'please' is the cherry on top — polite about the hot tub, imperious about the White House. Easily the episode's standout. Comparable to a strong 8.5+ anchor.

jokes.text: What? Uh, well, let's see. First of all, no. And then the other thing I really wanted to say was no.
jokes.setup: Jonah asks if Selina would consider letting Tom James speak at the rally.
jokes.punchline: Selina's no is structured as a formal two-part statement, both parts of which are 'no' — the bureaucratic framing of a flat refusal.
jokes.explanation: Excellent line. The formal framing ('first of all... the other thing I wanted to say') applied to a simple refusal is very Selina — she bureaucratizes even her petty rejections. Great economy and structure. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: I'm hearing a no.
jokes.setup: Selina has just delivered an elaborate double 'no.'
jokes.punchline: Jonah's tentative summary — 'I'm hearing a no' — as though it required interpretation, is a perfect understated beat.
jokes.explanation: Good button. Jonah treating an unambiguous refusal as needing careful interpretation is both a great callback and consistent with Jonah's self-deluding optimism.

jokes.text: Holy mother of moly.
jokes.setup: Something unexpected happens with the results.
jokes.punchline: 'Holy mother of moly' — Jonah's garbled version of 'Holy moly' crossed with 'Holy mother of God' — is an accidentally funny Jonahism.
jokes.explanation: A small but genuine Jonah verbal tic — he mangles idioms without realizing it. Consistent with his character.

jokes.text: Ohio? / No, for O'Brien. I should have said.
jokes.setup: Ohio is called — briefly presented as a Meyer win.
jokes.punchline: Kent's 'No, for O'Brien. I should have said' — delivered as a slight oversight rather than a critical failure of communication — is a perfectly Kent moment.
jokes.explanation: Great deadpan. Kent's complete equanimity about having just caused a moment of false jubilation — 'I should have said' as a mild correction — is perfect character comedy. The understatement of 'I should have said' for such a consequential omission is the joke.

jokes.text: Tie, like it's a tie? You mean, we tie? / You do. / What happens when there's a tie? / Everybody goes online to try to find out what happens if we get a tie. Way ahead of you. Shit, I'm just getting how to tie a tie.
jokes.setup: The possibility of an electoral tie is announced.
jokes.punchline: Ben rushes to look it up online and reports: 'I'm just getting how to tie a tie' — the search engine homophones of 'tie' (electoral) vs. 'tie' (neckwear) produces only neckwear results.
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay joke. The setup of an unprecedented electoral tie, the urgency of googling it, and then the punchline being neckwear tutorials is a brilliant structural comedy beat. The panic of the situation makes the mundane result funnier. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Is there a book? Like an old-fashioned... like a paper book?
jokes.setup: Everyone is desperately googling the tie scenario.
jokes.punchline: Selina's suggestion of a 'paper book' — clearly meaning a Constitution or law reference — is both an indication of how unprepared they are and a generationally funny suggestion.
jokes.explanation: Functional panic beat. 'A paper book' is a mildly funny specification, and the implication that nobody in the room has the Constitution to hand is good satire. Mild.

jokes.text: You do your best. You try to serve the people and then they just fuck you over. And you know why? Because they're ignorant and they're dumb as shit. And that, ladies and gentlemen, is democracy.
jokes.setup: Selina is frustrated by the tie and the constitutional confusion.
jokes.punchline: Selina's sudden breaking into what sounds like a speech — complete with 'ladies and gentlemen' — concluding with 'they're dumb as shit / that's democracy' is both satirically sharp and deeply character-revealing.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best and most thematically resonant jokes. Selina's aristocratic contempt for voters — expressed in a mock public address format — is the satirical spine of the whole show. 'And that, ladies and gentlemen, is democracy' as a summary is both genuinely funny and genuinely bleak. The formal 'ladies and gentlemen' applied to her private rant is perfect. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Why are there so many amendments? Get it right the first time, people.
jokes.setup: Staff are trying to figure out the 12th vs. 20th Amendment.
jokes.punchline: Selina's impatient dismissal of the amendment process — 'get it right the first time' — as though the Constitutional framers just weren't trying hard enough.
jokes.explanation: Sharp Selina line. Her irritation at the complexity of constitutional law is expressed as a quality-control complaint to the Founding Fathers. Nicely absurd.

jokes.text: What happens if it's a tie in the House? / Is it a dance-off?
jokes.setup: Explaining the constitutional process for resolving a House tie.
jokes.punchline: 'Is it a dance-off?' as a resolution mechanism for constitutional gridlock is a perfect absurdist non-sequitur from someone who clearly has no idea.
jokes.explanation: Brief but sharp absurdism. 'Dance-off' as a constitutional resolution mechanism is perfectly ridiculous, and the economy of delivery makes it land hard.

jokes.text: Well, I... I had literally no idea.
jokes.setup: Tom James realizes he might become president through a House tie — his own request to be VP now potentially lands him the presidency.
jokes.punchline: Tom's deadpan declaration of total ignorance — 'I had literally no idea' — while looking smug is the perfect expression of the joke: his scheming accidentally worked beyond his wildest plans.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character payoff. Whether Tom knew about the House tie mechanism all along (implied) or genuinely didn't (claimed), 'I had literally no idea' is hilarious in both readings. The pause — 'It's good to be prepared' — makes it even funnier. Rewatch bonus is high because re-watching Tom's earlier requests is completely recontextualized.

jokes.text: It's good to be prepared.
jokes.setup: Tom has just claimed he 'had literally no idea' about the tie scenario.
jokes.punchline: 'It's good to be prepared' — said after claiming ignorance, implying he did in fact know and plan for this.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The structure is perfect — claim ignorance, then immediately undercut it with 'it's good to be prepared.' It's a self-contradiction that functions as a confession, and the gap between the two statements is where the joke lives. Extremely quotable and a high rewatch value as a season payoff for Tom's ambitions. Economy is near-perfect. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: At least it'd be somebody from our team, ma'am. I mean, that's good, right?
jokes.setup: Selina realizes she might lose the presidency to her own VP.
jokes.punchline: Gary's attempt to comfort Selina by noting it would be 'somebody from our team' — suggesting Tom becoming president instead of her is just a minor reallocation — is perfectly clueless and immediately makes things worse.
jokes.explanation: Classic Gary. His comfort instinct fires at exactly the wrong moment with exactly the wrong thought. The idea that Selina would be mollified by her VP taking HER presidency because 'it's someone from our team' is purely Gary — both well-meaning and devastating. High character integration. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: I didn't mean to make her cry.
jokes.setup: Selina bursts into tears at the prospect of losing to Tom James.
jokes.punchline: Gary's small, devastated 'I didn't mean to make her cry' to camera — the talking head confession of someone who thought he was helping.
jokes.explanation: Perfect button on the Gary/team comment. His helpless guilt is very funny and very Gary. The brevity is ideal — nothing more needs to be said.

jokes.text: She's going down. / She's going down. / Don't touch her.
jokes.setup: Selina is overcome with emotion/stress and appears to be fainting or collapsing.
jokes.punchline: Multiple people announcing 'She's going down' with the urgency of a crisis, followed by 'Don't touch her' — the collective helplessness of the staff in the face of their president's breakdown.
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy beat. 'Don't touch her' as the instinctive response to the President collapsing is both funny (absurd reaction) and character-specific to the weird formality of Selina's world.

jokes.text: Oh, he's gone to talk to the crowd at the rally. / What? / The fuck he has.
jokes.setup: Selina asks where Tom James is; told he went to address the crowd.
jokes.punchline: 'The fuck he has' — Selina's instant transformation from emotional collapse to furious resolve is a great tonal whiplash joke.
jokes.explanation: Three words that do a lot of work. Selina's furious competitive instinct snapping back from tears is perfectly expressed in 'The fuck he has.' Economy is near-maximum.

jokes.text: No, I'll tell you what's unprecedented, Kent. A tie is unprecedented. So is becoming the first lady president. So is that jackoff becoming president through the back door.
jokes.setup: Kent says Selina going to the rally would be unprecedented.
jokes.punchline: Selina escalates through three things that are also unprecedented — ending on 'that jackoff becoming president through the back door' — the anger and the logic both accelerating.
jokes.explanation: Strong escalation speech. The three-part structure builds nicely, and 'through the back door' is a perfect crude double meaning for Tom ascending to the presidency by procedural trick. The fury is channeled into logical argument, which is pure Selina.

jokes.text: Okay? The rule book's been torn up now and America is wiping its nasty ass with it.
jokes.setup: Selina is justifying her decision to go to the rally despite it being unprecedented.
jokes.punchline: 'America is wiping its nasty ass with the rule book' — a magnificently crude image for constitutional chaos.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Selina line. The progression from 'rule book torn up' (cliché) to 'wiping its nasty ass with it' is a perfect Veep maneuver — taking a dead idiom and making it viscerally specific. Highly quotable. The word 'nasty' is doing important work. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: That fucking guy with his fucking charm and his fucking son and his fucking wheelchair with his spine all fucked up.
jokes.setup: Watching Tom James charm the crowd, Selina is seething.
jokes.punchline: Selina's monologue about Tom's advantages — culminating in resenting his son's disability ('his spine all fucked up') — is a shocking and precisely crafted expression of competitive psychopathy.
jokes.explanation: This is Selina Meyer as a character distilled. The escalating 'fucking' anaphora builds to the moral abyss of resenting Tom's son's spinal condition — not because she wishes him ill, but because it helps Tom's image. The show earns this by never endorsing Selina; it's character revelation through outrage. The rhythm of the speech is the craft. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: My grandmother survived the Dust Bowl. And before she died at the age of 89, she told me a secret that she kept for almost 50 years. The story concerns... Ladies and gentlemen, Tom James.
jokes.setup: Tom James is warming up the crowd with a personal story about his grandmother and the Dust Bowl — clearly building to an emotional payoff.
jokes.punchline: Jonah cuts him off mid-sentence to introduce him — 'Ladies and gentlemen, Tom James' — so we never hear the secret, and the elaborate setup pays off with nothing.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural joke. Tom builds audience anticipation with a genuine-seeming personal story — grandmother, Dust Bowl, secret kept 50 years — and Jonah's oblivious introduction vaporizes the payoff. The comedy is in the perfect timing of the interruption. The fact that we never hear the secret is funnier than any secret could be. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Do I know how to pick 'em? Yep.
jokes.setup: Tom takes the stage to enormous applause.
jokes.punchline: Tom's self-congratulatory 'Do I know how to pick 'em? Yep' about the crowd — as though he assembled them by choice — is the kind of confident, slightly absurd self-praise that defines his character.
jokes.explanation: Compact Tom James character beat. His absurd self-congratulation for crowd attendance is mildly funny and revealing.

jokes.text: Folks, you don't know her, but this is Karen Collins. And she has been a friend of mine for a while.
jokes.setup: Selina introduces her inner circle from the stage — after introducing her daughter and others.
jokes.punchline: 'You don't know her' — said of a friend introduced to the entire nation watching on election night, with a vague time qualifier ('for a while'). Karen's utter insignificance is publicly confirmed by her best friend.
jokes.explanation: The public dismissal of Karen is perfectly calibrated — Selina's introduction of her as 'you don't know her' to a national audience is both accurate and unintentionally brutal.

jokes.text: Amy Brookheimer who so successfully ran my campaign until she became unwell.
jokes.setup: Selina introduces Amy to the rally crowd publicly.
jokes.punchline: (Scored separately from the suite version — here delivered publicly, which makes it worse and funnier.) The public nature of 'became unwell' intensifies the joke.
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'became unwell' delivered now to a live national audience amplifies the joke significantly. Amy's reaction in the crowd would be visible. The public humiliation is sharper than the private version.

jokes.text: Welcome to my world.
jokes.setup: Kent says they're in 'a kind of purgatory' about the House result.
jokes.punchline: Bill's 'Welcome to my world' — he's been living in legal/prison purgatory all episode, and now everyone else gets to join him.
jokes.explanation: Three words, maximum efficiency. Bill's sardonic welcome to political purgatory is made funnier by the fact that his actual purgatory — probable imprisonment — is considerably worse than theirs. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: No, because I'm going to prison. Have I mentioned that?
jokes.setup: The team discusses next steps in the purgatory of the tie situation.
jokes.punchline: Bill interrupts the strategic discussion to re-center his prison narrative — 'Have I mentioned that?' — fully aware he has mentioned it repeatedly.
jokes.explanation: The running gag payoff — 'Have I mentioned that?' with full awareness that he has, in fact, mentioned it multiple times. The pseudo-casual way he checks in on his own prison arc mid-political-crisis is the joke. Good running gag management.

jokes.text: Can you stop thinking about yourself for one second? / Honestly. / No, because I'm going to prison. Have I mentioned that?
jokes.setup: Bill is accused of making the situation about himself.
jokes.punchline: His response — 'No, because I'm going to prison. Have I mentioned that?' — is both a logical defense and an ironic self-awareness about his constant prison references.
jokes.explanation: 'Have I mentioned that?' as a self-aware callback to his constant prison mentions is excellent. The fact that it's a completely valid excuse elevates it beyond just a catchphrase.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'll say. You might want to watch that if you become president.
jokes.setup: Tom James apologizes for overstepping by going to the rally.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'you might want to watch that if you become president' — delivered as advice to her own VP who might replace her — is simultaneously gracious and icily threatening.
jokes.explanation: Good Selina line. She simultaneously acknowledges the possibility of Tom becoming president and warns him — the tone is gracious on the surface and menacing underneath. Economical.

jokes.text: If that does happen, want to be my veep?
jokes.setup: Selina has just told Tom he might want to watch his behavior if he becomes president.
jokes.punchline: Tom asks Selina if she wants to be his VP — a perfect reversal of their relationship — delivered with complete casualness.
jokes.explanation: Excellent closer joke. The irony of the show's premise — Selina escaped the VP role to become President — being inverted so she might return to VP status is the thematic joke of the entire series crystallized in one line. Tom's casualness makes it worse. Very high rewatch value for understanding the show's arc. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Copy that.
jokes.setup: Selina has just told the man who may become president to go deal with the balloons.
jokes.punchline: Tom's 'Copy that' — said with the crisp professionalism of someone accepting a mission briefing, not being sent to count balloons.
jokes.explanation: Two words that perfectly close the episode. 'Copy that' — Tom accepting the balloon assignment with military-grade compliance — is both a character moment (Tom's graceful command presence) and a structural button (the episode ends with the future president going to deal with balloons). Maximum economy.

jokes.text: Why don't you go help them with the balloons?
jokes.setup: Tom has just offered Selina the VP slot.
jokes.punchline: Instead of responding to this enormously loaded question, Selina deflects him to balloon duty — the most presidential person in the room reduced to balloon handler.
jokes.explanation: Perfect final beat of the Tom/Selina rivalry. Selina neither accepts nor rejects his offer — she literally sends him away to deal with balloons, which is both a power move and an absurd deflation. The economy ('why don't you go help them with the balloons?') is near-perfect. Great episode closer.

tmdb_overview: On election night, Selina and her staff find their nerves growing frayed as each state result is called.
insight: Season finale delivers 73 jokes in 33 minutes but falls shy of elite status at 79.
